common_name: Champagne Loquat (White-Fleshed California Loquat, Golden Yellow Loquat)

planting.note: Plant container-grown grafted trees in spring in full to partial sun in well-drained soil; Champagne is the most widely grown and commercially important loquat variety in California and the standard variety in California commercial loquat production — it produces medium to large, pale yellow to golden-orange fruits with white to cream-colored flesh and an unusually mild, sweet, almost champagne-like delicate flavor that makes it the most refined-tasting commonly available loquat.

directions: Plant grafted tree in well-drained soil in full sun; loquats tolerate poor soils and occasional drought once established but produce much better fruit with regular moisture; thin fruit clusters to 3–5 per cluster for larger individual fruits; harvest when fruit is fully colored and slightly soft to pressure — loquats do not ripen further after picking. Champagne is the premier commercial loquat variety of the American Pacific Coast — the standard variety grown in California's loquat orchards and the type most commonly available in specialty markets and Asian grocery stores on the West Coast. The fruits are medium to large (1.5–2.5 inches), pear-shaped to rounded with a pale yellow to warm golden-yellow skin, and the flesh inside is distinctively cream-white to very pale yellow — unlike the deeper orange flesh of most loquat varieties. The white-fleshed characteristic is associated with a milder, more delicate, slightly acidic-sweet flavor profile that many tasters consider the finest among loquats, with a subtle fragrance sometimes compared to mild apricot or very ripe melon. The 'Champagne' name captures this refinement of flavor. Trees are vigorous and productive, blooming in fall-winter and fruiting in late winter to spring — one of the earliest fruit trees to ripen in the year, providing fresh fruit in February to April when little else is available. In zone 9a and the Gulf Coast, Champagne loquat reliably crops when winter temperatures stay above 26°F; the developing fruits are the most frost-sensitive part of the plant.

common_name: Big Jim Loquat (Large-Fruited Orange Loquat, Jim's Loquat)

planting.note: Plant grafted tree in spring in well-drained soil in full sun; Big Jim is one of the largest-fruited loquat varieties available, producing fruits that can reach 2–3 inches in length with a correspondingly smaller seed-to-flesh ratio than smaller-fruited varieties — ideal for home gardens where fruit quality and ease of eating are prioritized over heavy production per tree.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile soil; thin fruit clusters heavily (3–4 per cluster) to achieve the maximum individual fruit size that Big Jim is capable of; harvest when fruit is fully golden-orange and gives slightly to pressure; consume quickly as loquats have short shelf life (2–3 days at room temperature, 2 weeks refrigerated). Big Jim is a large-fruited loquat variety selected for the home garden market for its impressive individual fruit size — one of the largest-fruited loquat varieties available to home gardeners in the United States. The fruits are elongated, pear-shaped to oblong, with a warm orange skin and deep orange flesh that is sweet-tart and richly flavored — the classic loquat flavor in a package that provides more edible flesh per fruit than smaller varieties. Because loquats have large central seeds relative to their flesh, fruit size matters considerably in terms of eating efficiency: a large loquat like Big Jim offers a much better flesh-to-seed ratio than a small-fruited variety. The tree is moderately vigorous with an upright to spreading habit reaching 15–20 feet at maturity, blooming in fall (October–December in the Gulf South) with fragrant white flower clusters and ripening fruits from late winter through spring. Big Jim performs well in zones 8–10 and has been popular in Gulf Coast and California home gardens for generations. Heavy fruit thinning (to 3–4 fruits per cluster) is particularly important with Big Jim to achieve the maximum individual fruit size.

common_name: Premier Loquat (Florida Premier Loquat, Gulf Coast Orange Loquat)

planting.note: Plant grafted tree in spring in well-drained soil in full sun; Premier is the most widely planted commercial loquat variety in Florida and one of the best-adapted varieties for the humid Gulf Coast — selected for its reliable cropping under Florida and Gulf South conditions, good fruit quality, and consistent performance in the hot, humid subtropical climate of zones 9–10.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil, avoiding heavy clay or areas where water stands; Premier loquat blooms in October–November and ripens fruit in February–April in zone 9–10; thin fruit clusters to 4–6 per cluster for better-sized fruit; harvest when skin is fully golden-orange and slightly soft; store refrigerated for up to 2 weeks. Premier is the principal commercial loquat variety of Florida and one of the most reliable fruiting loquats for the Gulf Coast region, selected by the University of Florida extension system for its consistent performance in Florida's hot, humid, subtropical climate. The fruits are medium to medium-large, rounded to slightly pear-shaped, with warm orange to golden skin and orange flesh that is sweet, mildly acidic, and typically richer-flavored than Champagne (which is milder and more delicate). The variety bears consistently and prolifically in the Florida and Gulf Coast climate — more reliably than many California selections that may be adapted to a drier summer climate — and is the standard recommendation from UF IFAS Extension for home and commercial loquat production in Florida. Premier trees are moderately vigorous, reaching 15–20 feet at maturity, and are considered one of the best-adapted loquats for the humid subtropical conditions of zones 9–10 including the Gulf South, Louisiana, and coastal Texas.

common_name: Gold Nugget Loquat (Yellow-Fleshed Small-Seeded California Loquat)

planting.note: Plant grafted tree in spring in full sun with excellent drainage; Gold Nugget is valued for its small to medium fruits with unusually small seeds and deep yellow flesh — a combination that maximizes the ratio of edible flesh to seed and produces a distinctive golden-yellow color and rich, sweet flavor at full maturity; excellent as a backyard tree in zones 8–10.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun; Gold Nugget produces abundant crops of small to medium golden fruits from February through April in California and similarly timed in the Gulf Coast; the small seed size is a significant advantage for fresh eating and processing; no thinning is generally necessary due to the naturally smaller fruit size. Gold Nugget is a California loquat selection noted primarily for two characteristics: the unusually small seeds relative to fruit size, and the deep golden-yellow to amber flesh color that gives the variety its name. While most loquat varieties have relatively large seeds that reduce the proportion of edible flesh in each fruit, Gold Nugget produces a seed that is noticeably smaller than average, leaving more of the interior as eating flesh — a practical advantage for fresh eating and for any processing application (jams, preserves, sauces). The flesh color is a rich golden-yellow rather than the white of Champagne or the orange of Premier and Big Jim, and the flavor is sweet, richly aromatic, and quite juicy at peak ripeness. The tree is moderately vigorous and productive, and the smaller fruit size (typically 1–1.5 inches) means higher fruit counts per cluster compared to large-fruited varieties. In the Gulf Coast zone 9a, Gold Nugget blooms in fall and ripens in late winter to spring alongside other loquat varieties. An excellent choice for gardeners who want to maximize edible yield and minimize the frustration of handling large seeds.
sources: UC Cooperative Extension — Subtropical Fruit Crop Research

common_name: Advance Loquat (Early-Ripening Gulf Coast Orange-Fleshed Loquat)

planting.note: Plant grafted tree in spring in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Advance is one of the earliest-ripening loquat varieties available, with fruit typically mature 2–3 weeks ahead of standard varieties like Premier or Champagne — a significant advantage in the Gulf South where early spring frosts sometimes damage late-blooming varieties or where extended harvest overlapping with other fruit crops creates scheduling conflicts.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil; Advance loquat ripens earlier than most other varieties, often beginning in January–February in zone 9a, making it the first fresh tree fruit of the year in Gulf Coast gardens; fruits are medium, orange-fleshed, and sweet with moderate acidity; harvest sequentially as individual fruits ripen since this variety does not ripen all at once. Advance is an early-ripening loquat cultivar prized in Gulf Coast and Florida gardens for its ability to bring the first fresh homegrown tree fruit of the year to the table — typically ripening in January or February in zone 9a, 2–3 weeks ahead of the standard loquat season. This early ripening characteristic is agriculturally significant in two ways: it extends the effective loquat season (allowing home growers to plant multiple varieties for a longer fresh-fruit window from January through April), and it potentially reduces frost-damage risk to the developing fruit since early-ripening varieties complete their fruit development before the most severe January-February cold events. The fruits of Advance are medium-sized, rounded, with orange to golden-orange skin and firm, sweet orange flesh that is well-flavored without being as intensely aromatic as some other varieties. The tree is moderately vigorous and productive, and its earlier bloom time (September–October) compared to later-season varieties means some cross-pollination timing considerations when planting alongside other loquat varieties. Advance is a standard recommendation from Texas A&M AgriLife Extension for Gulf Coast home gardeners who want reliable loquat production.
